title: datavis-a2
text:
Import and clean the data (2%)

raw_dj <- read_excel("dja-performance-report-monthly.xlsx", skip = 5)

monthly_dj <- raw_dj |>
  filter(`Index Name` == "Dow Jones Industrial Average") |>
  transmute(
    date = as.Date(`Effective Date`, format = "%m/%d/%Y"),
    close_value = as.numeric(`Close Value`)
  )

head(monthly_dj, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       close_value
   <date>           <dbl>
 1 1896-05-29        40.6
 2 1896-06-30        36.2
 3 1896-07-31        32.0
 4 1896-08-31        32.0
 5 1896-09-30        36.0
 6 1896-10-31        39.5
 7 1896-11-30        41  
 8 1896-12-31        40.4
 9 1897-01-30        42.6
10 1897-02-27        41.7

title: datavis-a2
text:
Identify the gap months (3%)

monthly_dj_check <- monthly_dj |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(
    month = month(date),
    year = year(date),
    months_to_next_row = interval(date, lead(date)) %/% months(1)
  )

# The gap(s): any row where more than 1 month passes before the next entry
gap_start <- monthly_dj_check |>
  filter(months_to_next_row > 1)

gap_start

# A tibble: 1 × 5
  date       close_value month  year months_to_next_row
  <date>           <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>              <dbl>
1 1914-07-30        71.4     7  1914                  5


Running this on your actual file turns up exactly one gap: the row for 30 Jul 1914 jumps straight to 31 Dec 1914 (5 months skipped).
Historical reason (49 words): The New York Stock Exchange closed for over four months (31 Jul–12 Dec 1914) at the outbreak of World War I, fearing foreign investors would dump U.S. securities and drain gold reserves. No trading meant no DJIA prices during that window, creating the gap in the data.

title: datavis-a2
text:
Visualizing DJIA performance by month (10%)
Two new columns, explained simply:

rel_change_pct: “how much did this month’s close change vs last month’s, in percent?” It’s the classic (new - old) / old * 100 formula. We use lag() to grab “last month’s” value.
month_abb: turns the numeric month (1–12) into “Jan”, “Feb”, … and makes it an ordered factor. Without ordering, R would sort months alphabetically (Apr, Aug, Dec…) instead of calendar order — ordering fixes that so your x-axis reads Jan→Dec.


monthly_dj <- monthly_dj |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(
    rel_change_pct = (close_value / lag(close_value) - 1) * 100,
    month_abb = factor(month.abb[month(date)], levels = month.abb, ordered = TRUE)
  )

head(monthly_dj, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 4
   date       close_value rel_change_pct month_abb
   <date>           <dbl>          <dbl> <ord>    
 1 1896-05-29        40.6         NA     May      
 2 1896-06-30        36.2        -11.0   Jun      
 3 1896-07-31        32.0        -11.4   Jul      
 4 1896-08-31        32.0         -0.156 Aug      
 5 1896-09-30        36.0         12.8   Sep      
 6 1896-10-31        39.5          9.65  Oct      
 7 1896-11-30        41            3.72  Nov      
 8 1896-12-31        40.4         -1.34  Dec      
 9 1897-01-30        42.6          5.22  Jan      
10 1897-02-27        41.7         -2.00  Feb      



Visualization A: violin + box plot, with a line connecting medians
Analogy: a violin plot is like a boxplot that got its shape traced out — instead of just a box, you see the full “silhouette” of how the data is distributed (fat where there’s lots of data, thin where there’s little). We layer a slim boxplot inside the violin (like a box tucked inside its own shadow) by giving it a narrow width, so it stays within the violin’s outline.

monthly_dj_clean <- monthly_dj |> drop_na(rel_change_pct)

medians <- monthly_dj_clean |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarize(median_change = median(rel_change_pct), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(monthly_dj_clean, aes(x = month_abb, y = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "#a6cee3", color = "#1f78b4", trim = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white") +
  geom_line(data = medians, aes(x = month_abb, y = median_change, group = 1),
            color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = medians, aes(x = month_abb, y = median_change),
             color = "firebrick", size = 1.8) +
  labs(
    title = "DJIA Monthly Relative Change by Calendar Month",
    subtitle = "Violin shows full distribution shape; box shows quartiles; red line tracks the median",
    x = "Month", y = "Relative Change (%)"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = quantile(monthly_dj_clean$rel_change_pct, c(0.01, 0.99))) +
  theme_minimal()








(The coord_cartesian(ylim = ...) line just zooms the y-axis in on the middle 98% of data so a few extreme historical crash months — like Oct 1929 — don’t squash everything else flat. It doesn’t delete any data, it just changes the view, which is important since coord_cartesian — unlike ylim() — never drops points from the stats calculations.)


Visualization B: faceted frequency polygons + rug plot
Analogy: a frequency polygon is just a histogram redrawn as a connected line instead of bars — easier to compare shapes across many small panels. A “rug plot” adds little tick marks along the bottom showing exactly where each individual data point sits, like tick marks on a ruler.
facet_wrap() splits one big plot into a grid of small ones, one per group — here, one panel per month.

means <- monthly_dj_clean |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarize(mean_change = mean(rel_change_pct), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(monthly_dj_clean, aes(x = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_freqpoly(bins = 30, color = "#1f78b4", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_rug(sides = "b", alpha = 0.3, color = "#1f78b4") +
  geom_vline(data = means, aes(xintercept = mean_change),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "firebrick") +
  facet_wrap(~ month_abb, nrow = 4, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of DJIA Monthly Relative Change, by Month",
    subtitle = "Dashed red line = mean change for that month",
    x = "Relative Change (%)", y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: datavis-a2
text:
Import BTC and gold data (5%)
What tq_transmute() is doing, in plain terms: tq_get() downloads daily price data (like a spreadsheet download from Yahoo Finance). But we want monthly data, so tq_transmute() acts like a “summarizer” — to.monthly collapses each month’s daily prices down to one row per month.

ticker_btc <- "BTC-USD"
ticker_gold <- "GC=F" # Make sure this ticker is correct for Gold
start_date <- "2016-01-01"
end_date <- "2026-06-30"
# BTC Data
monthly_btc <- tq_get(ticker_btc, from = start_date, to = end_date) %>%
  drop_na(adjusted) %>%
  tq_transmute(select = adjusted, mutate_fun = to.monthly, indexAt = "lastof")
# Gold Data
monthly_gold <- tq_get(ticker_gold, from = start_date, to = end_date) %>%
  drop_na(adjusted) %>%
  tq_transmute(select = adjusted, mutate_fun = to.monthly, indexAt = "lastof")
head(monthly_btc, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       adjusted
   <date>        <dbl>
 1 2016-01-31     369.
 2 2016-02-29     438.
 3 2016-03-31     417.
 4 2016-04-30     448.
 5 2016-05-31     531.
 6 2016-06-30     673.
 7 2016-07-31     625.
 8 2016-08-31     575.
 9 2016-09-30     610.
10 2016-10-31     701.

head(monthly_gold, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       adjusted
   <date>        <dbl>
 1 2016-01-31    1116.
 2 2016-02-29    1234.
 3 2016-03-31    1234.
 4 2016-04-30    1289.
 5 2016-05-31    1215.
 6 2016-06-30    1318.
 7 2016-07-31    1349 
 8 2016-08-31    1307.
 9 2016-09-30    1313.
10 2016-10-31    1272.


Note: to.monthly (from the xts/quantmod package under the hood) will rename the value column — after running this, check names(monthly_btc) and names(monthly_gold). It’s very likely still called adjusted, but if it became something like adjusted.Adjusted, just rename it back with rename(adjusted = ...) before moving on.

title: datavis-a2
datavis-a2
text:
Volatility analysis: largest monthly drawdown (10%)
What a “drawdown” is, with an analogy: imagine your bank balance over time. Your “peak” is the highest it’s ever been. A “drawdown” at any later point is how far below that peak you’ve fallen, as a percentage. The maximum drawdown is your worst fall from grace — the biggest peak-to-valley drop before it recovered to a new high.
cummax() is the key trick: it walks through the price vector and records “the highest price seen so far” at every point — like a high-water mark on a flood wall. Then drawdown at each point = how far current price sits below that high-water mark.

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  max_drawdown <- abs(min(drawdown_pct))
  max_drawdown
}

# Sanity check: peak of 120 -> trough of 90 is the biggest fall = -25%...
# wait, actually walk through it: 100 -> 60 (peak so far 100, DD -40%)
#                                  60 -> 120 (new peak, DD 0%)
#                                  120 -> 90 (peak 120, DD -25%)
# so the worst drawdown in this vector is -40%, matching the expected answer.
prices_test <- c(100, 60, 120, 90)
compute_drawdown(prices_test)  # should print 40

[1] 40


Now we extend the function slightly so it also tells us when the peak and trough happened — useful for tying the numbers back to real events.

compute_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  trough_idx <- which.min(drawdown_pct)
  peak_idx <- max(which(prices[1:trough_idx] == running_max[trough_idx]))

  tibble(
    max_drawdown_pct = abs(drawdown_pct[trough_idx]),
    peak_date = dates[peak_idx],
    peak_price = prices[peak_idx],
    trough_date = dates[trough_idx],
    trough_price = prices[trough_idx]
  )
}

dj_since_2016 <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(date >= as.Date(start_date)) |>
  arrange(date)

drawdowns_dj <- compute_drawdown_details(dj_since_2016$date, dj_since_2016$close_value)
drawdowns_gold <- compute_drawdown_details(as.Date(monthly_gold$date), monthly_gold$adjusted)
drawdowns_btc <- compute_drawdown_details(as.Date(monthly_btc$date), monthly_btc$adjusted)

biggest_drawdown_dj <- drawdowns_dj$max_drawdown_pct
biggest_drawdown_gold <- drawdowns_gold$max_drawdown_pct
biggest_drawdown_btc <- drawdowns_btc$max_drawdown_pct

biggest_drawdown_gold

[1] 23.09913

biggest_drawdown_dj

[1] 23.20127

biggest_drawdown_btc

[1] 75.57435

drawdowns_gold

# A tibble: 1 × 5
  max_drawdown_pct peak_date  peak_price trough_date trough_price
             <dbl> <date>          <dbl> <date>             <dbl>
1             23.1 2026-02-28      5230. 2026-06-30         4022.

drawdowns_dj

# A tibble: 1 × 5
  max_drawdown_pct peak_date  peak_price trough_date trough_price
             <dbl> <date>          <dbl> <date>             <dbl>
1             23.2 2019-12-31     28538. 2020-03-31        21917.

drawdowns_btc

# A tibble: 1 × 5
  max_drawdown_pct peak_date  peak_price trough_date trough_price
             <dbl> <date>          <dbl> <date>             <dbl>
1             75.6 2017-12-31     14156. 2019-01-31         3458.


Writing your discussion paragraph: once you run the chunk above, look at the printed peak_date / trough_date for each asset and match them to real events, e.g.:

DJIA’s worst 2016–2026 drawdown very likely centers on the Feb–Mar 2020 COVID-19 crash.
Gold’s largest drawdown in this window is a plausible candidate for the 2022 Fed rate-hike cycle (a stronger dollar and higher bond yields make non-yielding gold less attractive).
BTC’s largest drawdown is a plausible candidate for either the Dec 2017–Dec 2018 “crypto winter” or the Nov 2021–Nov 2022 collapse following the Terra/Luna and FTX failures.

Check your actual peak_date/trough_date output against these before writing your final paragraph — don’t state a date you haven’t verified from your own results. A ~100-word paragraph should: (1) state the three drawdown percentages in increasing order, (2) name the peak/trough months per asset, (3) link each to its real-world trigger, and (4) conclude whether the ordering (Gold < DJIA < BTC in volatility) supports the hypothesis.

title: AAI1001 Data Engineering and Visualization
text:
# Packages required for Task 2
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.3     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.2     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(readxl)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyquant)

Warning: package 'tidyquant' was built under R version 4.6.1


Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod':
  method            from
  as.zoo.data.frame zoo 


Warning: package 'xts' was built under R version 4.6.1


Warning: package 'zoo' was built under R version 4.6.1


Warning: package 'quantmod' was built under R version 4.6.1


Warning: package 'TTR' was built under R version 4.6.1


Warning: package 'PerformanceAnalytics' was built under R version 4.6.1


── Attaching core tidyquant packages ─────────────────────── tidyquant 1.0.12 ──
✔ PerformanceAnalytics 2.1.0      ✔ TTR                  0.24.4
✔ quantmod             0.4.29     ✔ xts                  0.14.2
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyquant_conflicts() ──
✖ zoo::as.Date()                 masks base::as.Date()
✖ zoo::as.Date.numeric()         masks base::as.Date.numeric()
✖ dplyr::filter()                masks stats::filter()
✖ xts::first()                   masks dplyr::first()
✖ dplyr::lag()                   masks stats::lag()
✖ xts::last()                    masks dplyr::last()
✖ PerformanceAnalytics::legend() masks graphics::legend()
✖ quantmod::summary()            masks base::summary()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(scales)


Attaching package: 'scales'

The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    discard

The following object is masked from 'package:readr':

    col_factor

library(gt)

Warning: package 'gt' was built under R version 4.6.1






The source workbook contains five non-data rows before the column headings. The code below skips these rows, retains only the required variables, converts them to the requested classes, removes incomplete observations, and sorts the observations chronologically.

parse_excel_date <- function(x) {
  if (inherits(x, "Date")) {
    return(x)
  }

  if (inherits(x, c("POSIXct", "POSIXt"))) {
    return(as.Date(x))
  }

  mdy(as.character(x))
}

monthly_dj <- read_excel(
  "dja-performance-report-monthly.xlsx",
  skip = 5
) |>
  transmute(
    date = parse_excel_date(`Effective Date`),
    close_value = parse_number(as.character(`Close Value`))
  ) |>
  drop_na(date, close_value) |>
  arrange(date)

# Confirm the required classes
class(monthly_dj$date)

[1] "Date"

class(monthly_dj$close_value)

[1] "numeric"

# Display the first 10 rows
monthly_dj |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       close_value
   <date>           <dbl>
 1 1896-05-29        40.6
 2 1896-06-30        36.2
 3 1896-07-31        32.0
 4 1896-08-31        32.0
 5 1896-09-30        36.0
 6 1896-10-31        39.5
 7 1896-11-30        41  
 8 1896-12-31        40.4
 9 1897-01-30        42.6
10 1897-02-27        41.7





months_to_next_row is calculated from the difference in calendar year and calendar month. This method is preferred to a day-based duration because month-end dates have different numbers of days.

gap_months <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    month = month(date),
    year = year(date),
    months_to_next_row =
      (year(lead(date)) - year(date)) * 12 +
      (month(lead(date)) - month(date))
  ) |>
  filter(months_to_next_row > 1) |>
  select(date, close_value, month, year, months_to_next_row)

gap_months |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Starting Point of Missing DJIA Months**"),
    subtitle = "A gap is flagged when the next observation is more than one calendar month later"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    date = "Date",
    close_value = "DJIA Close",
    month = "Month",
    year = "Year",
    months_to_next_row = "Months to Next Row"
  ) |>
  fmt_date(columns = date, date_style = 3) |>
  fmt_number(columns = close_value, decimals = 2, use_seps = TRUE) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  tab_source_note(md("Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."))







Starting Point of Missing DJIA Months


A gap is flagged when the next observation is more than one calendar month later


Date
DJIA Close
Month
Year
Months to Next Row




Thu, Jul 30, 1914
71.42
7
1914
5



Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment.








The gap begins after July 1914. The NYSE closed on 31 July when World War I created fears that European investors would liquidate US securities and drain American gold reserves. Stock trading resumed in December 1914, explaining the missing August-November observations.
Source: Silber (2005), Journal of Financial Economics.



The relative monthly change is calculated as the percentage change from the previous monthly close. Month abbreviations are stored as an ordered factor so that all visualisations follow the calendar sequence from January to December.

monthly_dj <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    rel_change_pct = (close_value / lag(close_value) - 1) * 100,
    month_abb = factor(
      month(date),
      levels = 1:12,
      labels = month.abb,
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

monthly_statistics <- monthly_dj |>
  drop_na(rel_change_pct) |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarise(
    median_rel_change = median(rel_change_pct),
    mean_rel_change = mean(rel_change_pct),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




The violin plots show the complete distribution for each calendar month. Narrow box plots are placed inside the violin envelopes, while the connected points show how the median monthly return changes across the calendar year.

ggplot(monthly_dj, aes(x = month_abb, y = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    colour = "grey55",
    linewidth = 0.5,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  geom_violin(
    trim = TRUE,
    scale = "width",
    fill = "#9ECAE1",
    colour = "#08519C",
    alpha = 0.75,
    linewidth = 0.6,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.13,
    outlier.shape = NA,
    fill = "white",
    colour = "#08306B",
    alpha = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.55,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(
      x = month_abb,
      y = median_rel_change,
      group = 1
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    colour = "#D94801",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(
      x = month_abb,
      y = median_rel_change
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    shape = 21,
    size = 2.8,
    stroke = 0.7,
    fill = "#FDD0A2",
    colour = "#D94801"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "%", accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of DJIA Monthly Returns by Calendar Month",
    subtitle = "Box plots summarise the distributions; the orange line connects monthly medians",
    x = "Calendar month",
    y = "Relative monthly change",
    caption = "Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )











Each facet uses the same scales to support direct comparison. The dashed vertical line represents that month’s mean return, while the rug marks display the individual historical observations.

ggplot(monthly_dj, aes(x = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_freqpoly(
    bins = 24,
    colour = "#2C7FB8",
    linewidth = 0.75,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_rug(
    sides = "b",
    alpha = 0.30,
    colour = "#253494",
    length = grid::unit(0.025, "npc"),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(xintercept = mean_rel_change),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    colour = "#D94801",
    linewidth = 0.7,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(vars(month_abb), nrow = 4, ncol = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "%", accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "DJIA Monthly Return Distributions",
    subtitle = "Dashed orange lines indicate the historical mean for each calendar month",
    x = "Relative monthly change",
    y = "Frequency",
    caption = "Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey92", colour = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )















ticker_btc <- "BTC-USD"
ticker_gold <- "GC=F"
start_date <- "2016-01-01"
end_date <- "2026-06-30"

# Convert Bitcoin daily observations to monthly adjusted prices
monthly_btc <- tq_get(
  ticker_btc,
  from = start_date,
  to = end_date
) |>
  drop_na(adjusted) |>
  tq_transmute(
    select = adjusted,
    mutate_fun = to.period,
    period = "months"
  ) |>
  arrange(date)

# Convert gold-futures daily observations to monthly adjusted prices
monthly_gold <- tq_get(
  ticker_gold,
  from = start_date,
  to = end_date
) |>
  drop_na(adjusted) |>
  tq_transmute(
    select = adjusted,
    mutate_fun = to.period,
    period = "months"
  ) |>
  arrange(date)

monthly_btc |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       adjusted
   <date>        <dbl>
 1 2016-01-31     369.
 2 2016-02-29     438.
 3 2016-03-31     417.
 4 2016-04-30     448.
 5 2016-05-31     531.
 6 2016-06-30     673.
 7 2016-07-31     625.
 8 2016-08-31     575.
 9 2016-09-30     610.
10 2016-10-31     701.

monthly_gold |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       adjusted
   <date>        <dbl>
 1 2016-01-29    1116.
 2 2016-02-29    1234.
 3 2016-03-31    1234.
 4 2016-04-29    1289.
 5 2016-05-31    1215.
 6 2016-06-30    1318.
 7 2016-07-29    1349 
 8 2016-08-31    1307.
 9 2016-09-30    1313.
10 2016-10-31    1272.





All dates are converted to the first day of their month solely to align the three assets on one time axis. The comparison ends at the latest month available for all three assets, preventing unequal series lengths from misleading the reader.

djia_comparison <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(
    date >= as.Date(start_date),
    date <= as.Date(end_date)
  ) |>
  transmute(
    month = floor_date(date, unit = "month"),
    close_value,
    asset = "DJIA"
  )

btc_comparison <- monthly_btc |>
  transmute(
    month = floor_date(date, unit = "month"),
    close_value = adjusted,
    asset = "Bitcoin"
  )

gold_comparison <- monthly_gold |>
  transmute(
    month = floor_date(date, unit = "month"),
    close_value = adjusted,
    asset = "Gold"
  )

asset_prices <- bind_rows(
  djia_comparison,
  btc_comparison,
  gold_comparison
) |>
  distinct(asset, month, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    asset = factor(
      asset,
      levels = c("DJIA", "Bitcoin", "Gold")
    )
  )

latest_common_month <- asset_prices |>
  group_by(asset) |>
  summarise(last_month = max(month), .groups = "drop") |>
  summarise(latest_common_month = min(last_month)) |>
  pull(latest_common_month)

asset_prices <- asset_prices |>
  filter(
    month >= as.Date(start_date),
    month <= latest_common_month
  ) |>
  arrange(asset, month)

asset_prices |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 3
   month      close_value asset
   <date>           <dbl> <fct>
 1 2016-01-01      16466. DJIA 
 2 2016-02-01      16516. DJIA 
 3 2016-03-01      17685. DJIA 
 4 2016-04-01      17774. DJIA 
 5 2016-05-01      17787. DJIA 
 6 2016-06-01      17930. DJIA 
 7 2016-07-01      18432. DJIA 
 8 2016-08-01      18401. DJIA 
 9 2016-09-01      18308. DJIA 
10 2016-10-01      18142. DJIA 





A logarithmic price axis is used because the assets trade at very different numerical price levels. It preserves the original prices while allowing proportional rises and falls in all three curves to remain visible. Colour and line type are both mapped to asset, improving accessibility when the report is printed or viewed by readers with colour-vision deficiency.

ggplot(
  asset_prices,
  aes(
    x = month,
    y = close_value,
    colour = asset,
    linetype = asset
  )
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, lineend = "round") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "DJIA" = "#0072B2",
      "Bitcoin" = "#D55E00",
      "Gold" = "#E69F00"
    )
  ) +
  scale_linetype_manual(
    values = c(
      "DJIA" = "solid",
      "Bitcoin" = "longdash",
      "Gold" = "dotdash"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_date(
    date_breaks = "1 year",
    date_labels = "%Y",
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = label_dollar(accuracy = 1),
    breaks = breaks_log(n = 7)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Prices of the DJIA, Bitcoin, and Gold",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "January 2016 to ",
      format(latest_common_month, "%B %Y"),
      " | Logarithmic price axis"
    ),
    x = NULL,
    y = "Monthly closing price (USD, logarithmic scale)",
    colour = "Asset",
    linetype = "Asset",
    caption = "DJIA: supplied workbook. Bitcoin and gold: Yahoo Finance data retrieved with tidyquant."
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 1),
    linetype = guide_legend(nrow = 1)
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )











The function returns the percentage decline from the running peak at every observation. A value of zero indicates a new high; a larger value indicates a deeper fall below the previous peak.

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  if (!is.numeric(prices)) {
    stop("prices must be a numeric vector")
  }

  if (anyNA(prices)) {
    stop("prices must not contain missing values")
  }

  if (any(prices <= 0)) {
    stop("prices must be strictly positive")
  }

  running_peak <- cummax(prices)
  (running_peak - prices) / running_peak * 100
}

# Verification: the largest drawdown should be 40%
prices_test <- c(100, 60, 120, 90)
compute_drawdown(prices_test)

[1]  0 40  0 25

max(compute_drawdown(prices_test))

[1] 40



monthly_dj_decade <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(
    date >= as.Date(start_date),
    date <= as.Date(end_date)
  ) |>
  arrange(date)

drawdowns_gold <- compute_drawdown(monthly_gold$adjusted)
drawdowns_dj <- compute_drawdown(monthly_dj_decade$close_value)
drawdowns_btc <- compute_drawdown(monthly_btc$adjusted)

biggest_drawdown_gold <- max(drawdowns_gold)
biggest_drawdown_dj <- max(drawdowns_dj)
biggest_drawdown_btc <- max(drawdowns_btc)

biggest_drawdown_gold

[1] 23.09913

biggest_drawdown_dj

[1] 23.20127

biggest_drawdown_btc

[1] 75.57435


The helper function below identifies the peak that existed before the worst trough, ensuring that the reported peak always occurs before its corresponding trough.

get_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices, asset_name) {
  drawdown_pct <- compute_drawdown(prices)
  trough_index <- which.max(drawdown_pct)
  peak_index <- which.max(prices[seq_len(trough_index)])

  tibble(
    asset = asset_name,
    peak_month = floor_date(dates[peak_index], unit = "month"),
    peak_value = prices[peak_index],
    trough_month = floor_date(dates[trough_index], unit = "month"),
    trough_value = prices[trough_index],
    largest_drawdown_pct = drawdown_pct[trough_index]
  )
}

drawdown_summary <- bind_rows(
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_gold$date,
    monthly_gold$adjusted,
    "Gold"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_dj_decade$date,
    monthly_dj_decade$close_value,
    "DJIA"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_btc$date,
    monthly_btc$adjusted,
    "Bitcoin"
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    asset = factor(asset, levels = c("Gold", "DJIA", "Bitcoin"))
  ) |>
  arrange(asset)

drawdown_summary_display <- drawdown_summary |>
  mutate(
    peak_month = format(peak_month, "%b %Y"),
    trough_month = format(trough_month, "%b %Y")
  )

drawdown_summary_display |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Largest Monthly Peak-to-Trough Drawdowns**"),
    subtitle = "January 2016 to the latest common month"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    asset = "Asset",
    peak_month = "Peak Month",
    peak_value = "Peak Price",
    trough_month = "Trough Month",
    trough_value = "Trough Price",
    largest_drawdown_pct = "Largest Drawdown"
  ) |>
  fmt_currency(
    columns = c(peak_value, trough_value),
    currency = "USD",
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = largest_drawdown_pct,
    decimals = 1,
    pattern = "{x}%"
  ) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  tab_source_note(
    md("Drawdown = percentage fall from the running peak to a subsequent monthly trough.")
  )







Largest Monthly Peak-to-Trough Drawdowns


January 2016 to the latest common month


Asset
Peak Month
Peak Price
Trough Month
Trough Price
Largest Drawdown




Gold
Feb 2026
$5,230.50
Jun 2026
$4,022.30
23.1%


DJIA
Dec 2019
$28,538.44
Mar 2020
$21,917.16
23.2%


Bitcoin
Dec 2017
$14,156.40
Jan 2019
$3,457.79
75.6%



Drawdown = percentage fall from the running peak to a subsequent monthly trough.









# Store one-row results for concise inline reporting
gold_result <- drawdown_summary |>
  filter(asset == "Gold")

djia_result <- drawdown_summary |>
  filter(asset == "DJIA")

btc_result <- drawdown_summary |>
  filter(asset == "Bitcoin")

Gold recorded the smallest maximum drawdown (23.1%) from Feb 2026 to Jun 2026, moving from safe-haven demand to pressure from higher yields and a stronger US dollar. The DJIA fell 23.2% from Dec 2019 to Mar 2020 during the COVID-19 market shock. Bitcoin dropped 75.6% from Dec 2017 to Jan 2019 as a speculative boom reversed into a crypto winter. The ordering Gold < DJIA < Bitcoin supports the hypothesis, although drawdown measures only the single worst historical decline.
Event context: World Gold Council on rates and US-dollar headwinds · Federal Reserve on the COVID-19 equity-price plunge · BIS on crypto-winter losses

title: AAI1001 Data Engineering and Visualization
text:
# Packages required for Task 2
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──

✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.3     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.2     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(readxl)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyquant)

Warning: package 'tidyquant' was built under R version 4.6.1


Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod':
  method            from
  as.zoo.data.frame zoo 


Warning: package 'xts' was built under R version 4.6.1

✔ PerformanceAnalytics 2.1.0      ✔ TTR                  0.24.4
✔ quantmod             0.4.29     ✔ xts                  0.14.2

✖ zoo::as.Date()                 masks base::as.Date()
✖ zoo::as.Date.numeric()         masks base::as.Date.numeric()
✖ dplyr::filter()                masks stats::filter()
✖ xts::first()                   masks dplyr::first()
✖ dplyr::lag()                   masks stats::lag()
✖ xts::last()                    masks dplyr::last()
✖ PerformanceAnalytics::legend() masks graphics::legend()
✖ quantmod::summary()            masks base::summary()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(scales)


Attaching package: 'scales'

The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    discard

The following object is masked from 'package:readr':

    col_factor

library(gt)

Warning: package 'gt' was built under R version 4.6.1






The source workbook contains five non-data rows before the column headings. The code below skips these rows, retains only the required variables, converts them to the requested classes, removes incomplete observations, and sorts the observations chronologically.

parse_excel_date <- function(x) {
  if (inherits(x, "Date")) {
    return(x)
  }

  if (inherits(x, c("POSIXct", "POSIXt"))) {
    return(as.Date(x))
  }

  mdy(as.character(x))
}

monthly_dj <- read_excel(
  "dja-performance-report-monthly.xlsx",
  skip = 5
) |>
  transmute(
    date = parse_excel_date(`Effective Date`),
    close_value = parse_number(as.character(`Close Value`))
  ) |>
  drop_na(date, close_value) |>
  arrange(date)

# Confirm the required classes
class(monthly_dj$date)

[1] "Date"

class(monthly_dj$close_value)

[1] "numeric"

# Display the first 10 rows
monthly_dj |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 2
   date       close_value
   <date>           <dbl>
 1 1896-05-29        40.6
 2 1896-06-30        36.2
 3 1896-07-31        32.0
 4 1896-08-31        32.0
 5 1896-09-30        36.0
 6 1896-10-31        39.5
 7 1896-11-30        41  
 8 1896-12-31        40.4
 9 1897-01-30        42.6
10 1897-02-27        41.7





months_to_next_row is calculated from the difference in calendar year and calendar month. This method is preferred to a day-based duration because month-end dates have different numbers of days.

gap_months <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    month = month(date),
    year = year(date),
    months_to_next_row =
      (year(lead(date)) - year(date)) * 12 +
      (month(lead(date)) - month(date))
  ) |>
  filter(months_to_next_row > 1) |>
  select(date, close_value, month, year, months_to_next_row)

gap_months |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Starting Point of Missing DJIA Months**"),
    subtitle = "A gap is flagged when the next observation is more than one calendar month later"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    date = "Date",
    close_value = "DJIA Close",
    month = "Month",
    year = "Year",
    months_to_next_row = "Months to Next Row"
  ) |>
  fmt_date(columns = date, date_style = 3) |>
  fmt_number(columns = close_value, decimals = 2, use_seps = TRUE) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  tab_source_note(md("Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."))







Starting Point of Missing DJIA Months


A gap is flagged when the next observation is more than one calendar month later


Date
DJIA Close
Month
Year
Months to Next Row




Thu, Jul 30, 1914
71.42
7
1914
5



Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment.








The gap begins after July 1914. The NYSE closed on 31 July when World War I created fears that European investors would liquidate US securities and drain American gold reserves. Stock trading resumed in December 1914, explaining the missing August-November observations.
Source: Silber (2005), Journal of Financial Economics.



The relative monthly change is calculated as the percentage change from the previous monthly close. Month abbreviations are stored as an ordered factor so that all visualisations follow the calendar sequence from January to December.

monthly_dj <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    rel_change_pct = (close_value / lag(close_value) - 1) * 100,
    month_abb = factor(
      month(date),
      levels = 1:12,
      labels = month.abb,
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

monthly_statistics <- monthly_dj |>
  drop_na(rel_change_pct) |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarise(
    median_rel_change = median(rel_change_pct),
    mean_rel_change = mean(rel_change_pct),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




The violin plots show the complete distribution for each calendar month. Narrow box plots are placed inside the violin envelopes, while the connected points show how the median monthly return changes across the calendar year.

ggplot(monthly_dj, aes(x = month_abb, y = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    colour = "grey55",
    linewidth = 0.5,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  geom_violin(
    trim = TRUE,
    scale = "width",
    fill = "#9ECAE1",
    colour = "#08519C",
    alpha = 0.75,
    linewidth = 0.6,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.13,
    outlier.shape = NA,
    fill = "white",
    colour = "#08306B",
    alpha = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.55,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(
      x = month_abb,
      y = median_rel_change,
      group = 1
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    colour = "#D94801",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(
      x = month_abb,
      y = median_rel_change
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    shape = 21,
    size = 2.8,
    stroke = 0.7,
    fill = "#FDD0A2",
    colour = "#D94801"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "%", accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of DJIA Monthly Returns by Calendar Month",
    subtitle = "Box plots summarise the distributions; the orange line connects monthly medians",
    x = "Calendar month",
    y = "Relative monthly change",
    caption = "Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )











Each facet uses the same scales to support direct comparison. The dashed vertical line represents that month’s mean return, while the rug marks display the individual historical observations.

ggplot(monthly_dj, aes(x = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_freqpoly(
    bins = 24,
    colour = "#2C7FB8",
    linewidth = 0.75,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_rug(
    sides = "b",
    alpha = 0.30,
    colour = "#253494",
    length = grid::unit(0.025, "npc"),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(xintercept = mean_rel_change),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    colour = "#D94801",
    linewidth = 0.7,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(vars(month_abb), nrow = 4, ncol = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "%", accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "DJIA Monthly Return Distributions",
    subtitle = "Dashed orange lines indicate the historical mean for each calendar month",
    x = "Relative monthly change",
    y = "Frequency",
    caption = "Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey92", colour = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )















ticker_btc <- "BTC-USD"
ticker_gold <- "GC=F"
start_date <- "2016-01-01"
end_date <- "2026-06-30"

# Convert Bitcoin daily observations to monthly adjusted prices
monthly_btc <- tq_get(
  ticker_btc,
  from = start_date,
  to = end_date
) |>
  drop_na(adjusted) |>
  tq_transmute(
    select = adjusted,
    mutate_fun = to.period,
    period = "months"
  ) |>
  arrange(date)

# Convert gold-futures daily observations to monthly adjusted prices
monthly_gold <- tq_get(
  ticker_gold,
  from = start_date,
  to = end_date
) |>
  drop_na(adjusted) |>
  tq_transmute(
    select = adjusted,
    mutate_fun = to.period,
    period = "months"
  ) |>
  arrange(date)

monthly_btc |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

All dates are converted to the first day of their month solely to align the three assets on one time axis. The comparison ends at the latest month available for all three assets, preventing unequal series lengths from misleading the reader.

djia_comparison <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(
    date >= as.Date(start_date),
    date <= as.Date(end_date)
  ) |>
  transmute(
    month = floor_date(date, unit = "month"),
    close_value,
    asset = "DJIA"
  )

btc_comparison <- monthly_btc |>
  transmute(
    month = floor_date(date, unit = "month"),

  )

gold_comparison <- monthly_gold |>

asset_prices <- bind_rows(

  gold_comparison
) |>
  distinct(asset, month, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    asset = factor(
      asset,
      levels = c("DJIA", "Bitcoin", "Gold")
    )
  )

latest_common_month <- asset_prices |>
  group_by(asset) |>
  summarise(last_month = max(month), .groups = "drop") |>
  summarise(latest_common_month = min(last_month)) |>
  pull(latest_common_month)

asset_prices <- asset_prices |>
  filter(
    month >= as.Date(start_date),
    month <= latest_common_month
  ) |>
  arrange(asset, month)

asset_prices |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 3
   month      close_value asset
   <date>           <dbl> <fct>
 1 2016-01-01      16466. DJIA 
 2 2016-02-01      16516. DJIA

A logarithmic price axis is used because the assets trade at very different numerical price levels. It preserves the original prices while allowing proportional rises and falls in all three curves to remain visible. Colour and line type are both mapped to asset, improving accessibility when the report is printed or viewed by readers with colour-vision deficiency.

ggplot(
  asset_prices,
  aes(
    x = month,
    y = close_value,
    colour = asset,
    linetype = asset
  )
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, lineend = "round") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "DJIA" = "#0072B2",
      "Bitcoin" = "#D55E00",
      "Gold" = "#E69F00"
    )
  ) +
  scale_linetype_manual(
    values = c(
      "DJIA" = "solid",
      "Bitcoin" = "longdash",
      "Gold" = "dotdash"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_date(
    date_breaks = "1 year",
    date_labels = "%Y",
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = label_dollar(accuracy = 1),
    breaks = breaks_log(n = 7)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Prices of the DJIA, Bitcoin, and Gold",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "January 2016 to ",
      format(latest_common_month, "%B %Y"),
      " | Logarithmic price axis"
    ),
    x = NULL,
    y = "Monthly closing price (USD, logarithmic scale)",
    colour = "Asset",
    linetype = "Asset",
    caption = "DJIA: supplied workbook. Bitcoin and gold: Yahoo Finance data retrieved with tidyquant."
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 1),
    linetype = guide_legend(nrow = 1)
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )











The function returns the percentage decline from the running peak at every observation. A value of zero indicates a new high; a larger value indicates a deeper fall below the previous peak.

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  if (!is.numeric(prices)) {
    stop("prices must be a numeric vector")
  }

  if (anyNA(prices)) {
    stop("prices must not contain missing values")
  }

  if (any(prices <= 0)) {
    stop("prices must be strictly positive")
  }

  running_peak <- cummax(prices)
  (running_peak - prices) / running_peak * 100
}

# Verification: the largest drawdown should be 40%
prices_test <- c(100, 60, 120, 90)
compute_drawdown(prices_test)

[1]  0 40  0 25

max(compute_drawdown(prices_test))

[1] 40



monthly_dj_decade <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(
    date >= as.Date(start_date),
    date <= as.Date(end_date)
  ) |>
  arrange(date)

drawdowns_gold <- compute_drawdown(monthly_gold$adjusted)
drawdowns_dj <- compute_drawdown(monthly_dj_decade$close_value)
drawdowns_btc <- compute_drawdown(monthly_btc$adjusted)

biggest_drawdown_gold <- max(drawdowns_gold)
biggest_drawdown_dj <- max(drawdowns_dj)
biggest_drawdown_btc <- max(drawdowns_btc)

The helper function below identifies the peak that existed before the worst trough, ensuring that the reported peak always occurs before its corresponding trough.

get_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices, asset_name) {
  drawdown_pct <- compute_drawdown(prices)
  trough_index <- which.max(drawdown_pct)
  peak_index <- which.max(prices[seq_len(trough_index)])

  tibble(
    asset = asset_name,
    peak_month = floor_date(dates[peak_index], unit = "month"),
    peak_value = prices[peak_index],
    trough_month = floor_date(dates[trough_index], unit = "month"),
    trough_value = prices[trough_index],
    largest_drawdown_pct = drawdown_pct[trough_index]
  )
}

drawdown_summary <- bind_rows(
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_gold$date,
    monthly_gold$adjusted,
    "Gold"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_dj_decade$date,
    monthly_dj_decade$close_value,
    "DJIA"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_btc$date,
    monthly_btc$adjusted,
    "Bitcoin"
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    asset = factor(asset, levels = c("Gold", "DJIA", "Bitcoin"))
  ) |>
  arrange(asset)

drawdown_summary_display <- drawdown_summary |>
  mutate(
    peak_month = format(peak_month, "%b %Y"),
    trough_month = format(trough_month, "%b %Y")
  )

drawdown_summary_display |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Largest Monthly Peak-to-Trough Drawdowns**"),
    subtitle = "January 2016 to the latest common month"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    asset = "Asset",
    peak_month = "Peak Month",
    peak_value = "Peak Price",
    trough_month = "Trough Month",
    trough_value = "Trough Price",
    largest_drawdown_pct = "Largest Drawdown"
  ) |>
  fmt_currency(
    columns = c(peak_value, trough_value),
    currency = "USD",
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = largest_drawdown_pct,
    decimals = 1,
    pattern = "{x}%"
  ) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  tab_source_note(
    md("Drawdown = percentage fall from the running peak to a subsequent monthly trough.")
  )

Drawdown = percentage fall from the running peak to a subsequent monthly trough.









# Store one-row results for concise inline reporting
gold_result <- drawdown_summary |>
  filter(asset == "Gold")

djia_result <- drawdown_summary |>
  filter(asset == "DJIA")

btc_result <- drawdown_summary |>
  filter(asset == "Bitcoin")

Gold recorded the smallest maximum drawdown (23.1%) from Feb 2026 to Jun 2026, moving from safe-haven demand to pressure from higher yields and a stronger US dollar. The DJIA fell 23.2% from Dec 2019 to Mar 2020 during the COVID-19 market shock. Bitcoin dropped 75.6% from Dec 2017 to Jan 2019 as a speculative boom reversed into a crypto winter. The ordering Gold < DJIA < Bitcoin supports the hypothesis, although drawdown measures only the single worst historical decline.
Event context: World Gold Council on rates and US-dollar headwinds · Federal Reserve on the COVID-19 equity-price plunge · BIS on crypto-winter losses
